Introduction
To compare the theory of vanishing cycles of exposé XIII with its transcendental analogue, over \(\mathbb C\), we will proceed in two steps.

a) We will give a transcendental formalism modeled on the formalism of exposé XIII, and will prove a comparison theorem by the usual methods (already used to prove the finiteness theorem in XIII 2.3).
b) In the case of isolated singularities, we will make explicit in more concrete terms what the transcendental formalism introduced means.
Besides the comparison theorem, this exposé contains a proof of the Picard-Lefschetz formula (transcendental case).
In § 4, we give a preliminary result for the Hodge theory of vanishing cycles. This paragraph will not be used in the remainder of the seminar.
§ 1 Transcendental formalism of vanishing cycles
1.1. The fundamental group of the punctured disk : sign conventions.

We will have to consider punctured disks \(D^*\), and their fundamental group \(\mathbb Z\). Since this group is abelian, sign ambiguities are liable to appear. One eliminates them by forgetting that it is.

1.1.1. For \(X\) a good connected topological space and \(x\in X\), the fundamental group has as elements the homotopy classes of loops based at \(x\) ; if \(\alpha,\beta\in\pi_1(X,x)\) are represented by loops \(a,b\) then \(\alpha\beta\) is represented by the loop obtained by following \(b\) with \(a\).

1.1.2. Let \(F\) be a locally constant sheaf on \(X\). For every path \(a:[0,1]\to X\), the inverse image \(a^*F\) of \(F\) on \([0,1]\) is a locally constant sheaf, hence constant, whence a bijection
\[ a:F_{a(0)}\mathop{\longleftarrow}\limits^\sim H^0([0,1],a^*F)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim F_{a(1)}, \]

which depends only on the homotopy class of \(a\). By this construction, \(\pi_1(X,x)\) acts (on the left) on \(F_x\). The functor \(F\mapsto F_x\) is an equivalence of the category of locally constant sheaves on \(X\) with that of sets equipped with an action of \(\pi_1(X,x)\).

1.1.3. The \(\pi_1(X,x)\) for variable \(x\) are the stalks of a locally constant sheaf of groups \(\pi_1(X)\) which acts on every locally constant sheaf \(F\). If \(\pi_1(X,x)\) is abelian, \(\pi_1(X)\) is constant, with value denoted \(\pi_1(X)\).

1.1.4. The group \(\pi_1(X,x)\) acts on the universal covering \(p:(\widetilde X,\widetilde x)\to(X,x)\) of \((X,x)\) : if one regards this covering as an étalé space, hence a sheaf, on \(X\) (the sheaf of its local sections), it corresponds by 1.1.2 to \(\pi_1(X,x)\), equipped with \(e\), on which \(\pi_1(X,x)\) acts by left translation ; the group \(\pi_1(X,x)\) acts on this \(\pi_1\)-set (by automorphisms of \(\pi_1\)-sets) : \(\sigma\in\pi_1(X,x)\) acts by \(x\mapsto x\sigma^{-1}\).
This construction identifies \(\pi_1(X,x)\) with \(\operatorname{Aut}_X(\widetilde X)\).
If \(F\) is locally constant on \(X\), the bijection
\[ \tag{1.1.4.1}H^0(\widetilde X,p^*F)\longrightarrow(p^*F)_{\widetilde x}=F_x \]

commutes with the action of \(\pi_1(X,x)\), acting on the left-hand side by transport of structure, via its action on \(\widetilde X\), and on the right-hand side by 1.1.2. The equivalence 1.1.2 can be reformulated by saying that \(F\mapsto(\widetilde X\mapsto H^0(\widetilde X,p^*F))\) is an equivalence of the category of locally constant sheaves on \(X\) with that of functors
\[ (\text{universal covers of }X)\longrightarrow(\mathrm{Ens}). \]

1.1.5. If \(\pi_1(X,x)\) is abelian, hence independent of \(x\), the action 1.1.4 of \(\pi_1(X)\) on \(\widetilde X\) does not depend on the base point : \(\pi_1(X)\) acts on every universal covering \(\widetilde X\) of \(X\).
One should beware that this action (which will be the only one considered) corresponds to the inverse of the action 1.1.3.

1.1.6. Let \(D\) be a disk, i.e. a topological space homeomorphic to the standard disk \(\{z\in\mathbb C\mid |z|<1\}\). Let \(0\in D\) and \(D^*=D-\{0\}\). Suppose \(D\) equipped with a structure of analytic manifold, hence oriented. The fundamental group \(\pi_1(D^*)\) is then canonically isomorphic to \(\mathbb Z\) : for the standard disk, \(0=0\) and \(t\in D^*\), one takes as positive generator of \(\pi_1(D^*,t)\) the loop \(x\mapsto\exp(2\pi i x)t\).
1.1.7. Let \(z:D\to\mathbb C\) be a uniformizer (\((dz)_0\ne0\)). Let \(\widetilde D^*\) be a universal covering of \(D^*\). We denote by \(\log z\) any function on \(\widetilde D^*\) such that
\[ \exp(\log z)=z. \]

We set, for \(\alpha\in\mathbb C\), \(z^\alpha=\exp(\alpha\log z)\). If \(T\) is the positive generator of \(\pi_1(D^*)\), acting on \(\widetilde D^*\) by 1.1.5 and on the functions on \(\widetilde D^*\) by transport of structure, we have
\[ \tag{1.1.7.1}T\log(z)=\log(z)+2\pi i \] \[ \tag{1.1.7.2}Tz^\alpha=\exp(2\pi i\alpha)z^\alpha. \]

1.1.8. Let \(D\) be a disk, \(0\in D\) and \(D^*=D-\{0\}\). For \(D'\subset D\) a smaller disk, with \(0\in D'\), restriction to \(D'^*\) is an equivalence of the category of locally constant sheaves on \(D^*\) with that of locally constant sheaves on \(D'^*\). For \(t\in D'^*\), we indeed have \(\pi_1(D'^*,t)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim\pi_1(D^*,t)\).

Suppose that \(D\) is equipped with a structure of differentiable manifold, let \(T\) be the tangent space to \(D\) at \(0\), and \(T^*=T-\{0\}\). For \(D'\subset D\) as above, and \(f:D'\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow T\) with \(f(0)=0\) and \((df)_0=\operatorname{Id}\), the composite
\[ (\text{restriction to }D')^{-1}\circ f^* \]

is an equivalence \([f]\) of the category of locally constant sheaves on \(T^*\) with that of locally constant sheaves on \(D^*\). Up to canonical isomorphism, it depends only on the germ of \(f\) at \(0\), this germ satisfying \((df)_0=\operatorname{Id}\). A path from one such germ to another defines an isomorphism of equivalences and two homotopic paths the same isomorphism ; the set of germs considered being contractible (retraction onto \(f_0:(1-\lambda)f_0+\lambda f\)), one obtains an equivalence, well defined up to unique isomorphism, of the category of locally constant sheaves on \(T^*\) with that of locally constant sheaves on \(D^*\).

1.1.9. For \(D\) a disk, \(0\in D\), \(D^*=D-\{0\}\) and \(p:\widetilde D^*\to D^*\) a universal covering of \(D^*\), we will denote by \(p:\widetilde D\to D\) the topological space over \(D\) deduced from \(\widetilde D^*\) by adjoining a point \(0\), so that

1. a) \(\widetilde D^*\) is open in \(\widetilde D\) ;
  2. b) \(p:\widetilde D\to D\) extends \(p:\widetilde D^*\to D^*\) and \(p(0)=0\) ;
  3. c) the \(p^{-1}(U)\), for \(U\) a neighborhood of \(0\) in \(D\), forming a fundamental system of neighborhoods of \(0\) in \(\widetilde D\).

1.1.10. Let \(X\) be a nonsingular complex algebraic curve, \(0\in X\) and \(D\) a disk neighborhood of \(0\) in \(X\). The ring of germs at \(0\) of holomorphic functions on \(D\), algebraic over the field \(K(X)\) of rational functions on \(X\), is the henselization \(\mathcal O_0^h\) of the local ring \(\mathcal O_0\) of \(X\) at \(0\). Let \(\widetilde D^*\) be a universal covering of \(D^*=D-\{0\}\). The field of germs at \(0\in\widetilde D\) of meromorphic functions on \(\widetilde D^*\), algebraic over \(K(X)\), is an algebraic closure \(\overline K\) of \(K(X)\) (and hence of the fraction field \(K_0^h\) of \(\mathcal O_0^h\)).
The group \(\pi_1(D^*)\) of \(D\)-automorphisms of \(\widetilde D^*\) (1.1.5) acts by transport of structure on \(\overline K\), whence
\[ \tag{1.1.10.1}\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{{\pi_1(D^*)}\ar[r]\ar@{=}[d]&{\operatorname{Gal}(\overline K/K_0^h)}\ar@{=}[d]\\\mathbb Z\ar[r]&{\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)}}. \]

It follows from (1.1.7.2) that this diagram commutes with the natural inclusion (on \(\mathbb C\)) of \(\mathbb Z\) into \(\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)\), the composite of the inclusion of \(\mathbb Z\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\widehat{\mathbb Z}\) and the isomorphism \(\widehat{\mathbb Z}\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim\widehat{\mathbb Z}(1)\), the projective limit of the isomorphisms \(\exp(\frac{2\pi i}{n}k):\mathbb Z/n\to\mu_n\).

1.1.11. Each sheaf \(F\) on \(\operatorname{Spec}(K_0^h)\) defines a locally constant sheaf on \(D^*\) : one uses the equivalence 1.1.4 and, to each universal covering \(\widetilde D^*\) of \(D^*\), one associates \(F_{\overline K}\) for the \(\overline K\) constructed in 1.1.10.
1.2. Sheaves of sets.

1.2.1. Let \(D\) be a disk (1.1.6), \(0\in D\) and \(D^*=D-\{0\}\). We will denote by \(\mathscr D^*\) the topos of locally constant sheaves of sets on \(D^*\). By 1.1.8, this topos does not « change » if one replaces \(D\) by a smaller disk \(D'\), containing \(0\), nor, for \(D\) equipped with a structure of differentiable manifold, if one replaces \(D\) by its tangent space at \(0\).
For \(t\in D^*\), the functor \(F\longmapsto F_t\) identifies this topos with the topos of sets equipped with an action of \(\pi_1(D^*)\) (1.1.2). By (1.1.4), the functor
\[ F\longmapsto(\widetilde D^*\longmapsto H^0(\widetilde D^*,p^*F)) \]
identifies it with the topos of functors
\[ (\text{universal covers of }D^*)\longrightarrow(\mathrm{Ens}), \]
and these identifications are related by 1.1.4.1.

1.2.2. Let \(F\) be a sheaf on \(D\) whose restriction to \(D^*\) is locally constant. There exists one and only one morphism of sheaves \(F_0\xrightarrow{\alpha}F\) which is the identity at \(0\). As explained in XIII 0.1 (for \(X=(P^1_t)\)), the functor
\[ F\longmapsto(F_0,F\text{ restricted to }D^*,\alpha) \]
identifies these sheaves with the triples formed of a set \(F_0\), a locally constant sheaf \(F_\eta\) on \(D^*\) and \(\alpha\) :
\[ (\text{constant sheaf with value }F_0\text{ on }D^*)\longrightarrow(\text{sheaf }F_\eta). \]

1.2.3. We will denote by \(\mathscr D\) the topos of sheaves of sets on \(D\) whose restriction to \(D^*\) is locally constant. The inclusion of \(\mathscr D\) into the sheaves on \(D\) is the inverse image morphism of a morphism of topoi \(D\to\mathscr D\) which fits into a commutative diagram
As in 1.1.1, one verifies that this topos does not change when one replaces \(D\) by a smaller disk or by the tangent space to \(D\) at \(0\).
1.2.4. Let \(Y\) be a topological space. The product topos \(Y\times\mathscr D\) admits the following three descriptions (for their equivalence, see XIII 0.1)

1. a) \(Y\times\mathscr D\) is the topos of sheaves \(F\) on \(Y\times D\) satisfying
    (*) \(D^*\) is covered by open sets \(U\) such that \(F|Y\times U\) is the inverse image of a sheaf on \(Y\).
  2. b) Let \(t\in D^*\). The topos \(Y\times\mathscr D\) is the topos of triples \((F_0,F_t,\alpha)\) where
    \(\alpha)\) \(F_0\) is a sheaf on \(Y\) ;
    \(\beta)\) \(F_t\) is a sheaf on \(Y\), equipped with an action of \(\pi_1(D^*)\) ;
    \(\gamma)\) \(\alpha\) is a morphism from \(F_0\) into \(F_t\), with image contained in the invariants.
  3. c) The topos \(Y\times\mathscr D\) is the topos of triples \((F_0,F_\eta,\alpha)\) where
    \(\alpha')\) \(F_0\) is a sheaf on \(Y\) ;
    \(\beta')\) \(F_\eta\) is a functor, with values denoted \(F_{\widetilde\eta}\),
    \[ (\text{universal covers of }D^*)\longrightarrow(\text{sheaves on }Y) ; \] \(\gamma')\) \(\alpha\) is a morphism from \(F_0\) (the constant functor with value \(F_0\) from the universal coverings of \(D^*\) into the sheaves on \(Y\)) into \(F_\eta\).
It matters little to us, moreover, whether this topos really is the product topos of \(Y\) and \(\mathscr D\). One likewise defines \(Y\times\mathscr D^*\) as being, at one's choice

1. a*) the topos of sheaves on \(Y\times D^*\) satisfying (*) ;
  2. b*) (for \(t\in D^*\)) the topos of sheaves \(F_t\) on \(Y\), equipped with an action of \(\pi_1(D^*)\) ;
  3. c*) the topos of functors \[ (\text{universal covers of }D^*)\longrightarrow(\text{sheaves on }Y). \]

1.2.5. The topos \(Y\times\mathscr D\) has the same functoriality properties in \(Y\) as the topoi \(Y\mathbin{\mathop\times_S}S\) of exposé XIII, and we will refrain from listing them. Let us simply cite, to fix the notations, the diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=3em@R=3em{ Y\ar[d]&Y\ar[l]_{\mathrm{sp}}\ar[d]\ar@{^{(}->}[r]^i&Y\times\mathscr D\ar[d]&Y\times\mathscr D^*\ar[d]\ar@{_{(}->}[l]_j\\ (P^1_t)&\{0\}\ar@{^{(}->}[r]^i&\mathscr D&\mathscr D^*\ar@{_{(}->}[l]_j } \]
1.2.6. Let \(K\) be a complex of abelian sheaves on \(Y\times\mathscr D\). One proceeds as in XIII 1.4.2 to deduce from \(K\) a distinguished triangle in \(K(Y\times\mathscr D^*)\)
\[ \xymatrix@C=5em@R=3em{&\Phi(K)\ar[dl]\ar[dr]&\\ \mathrm{sp}^*K_0\ar[rr]&&K_\eta.} \]
This construction passes to the derived category and defines a functor
\[ D(Y\times\mathscr D)\longrightarrow(\text{distinguished triangles of }D(Y\times\mathscr D^*)). \]

Suppose \(D\) oriented (for example complex analytic) and let \(T\) be the positive generator of \(\pi_1(D^*)\) (1.1.6). Let \(K\in\operatorname{Ob}D(Y\times\mathscr D)\). One defines as in XIII 1.4.3 the variation \(V=\operatorname{Var}(T)\)
\[ V:\Phi(K)\longrightarrow K_\eta. \]
The endomorphism \(T-\operatorname{Id}\) of \(K_\eta\) is the composite
\[ T-\operatorname{Id}:K_\eta\longrightarrow\Phi(K)\longrightarrow K_\eta. \]
1.2.7. Let \(D\) be a disk, \(0\in D\), \(D^*=D-\{0\}\), \(f:X\to D\) a continuous map and \(X_0=f^{-1}(0)\). We propose to define a functor
\[ \Psi:(\text{sheaves on }X)\longrightarrow(\text{sheaves on }X_0\times\mathscr D). \]
Let \(\widetilde D^*\) be a universal covering of \(D^*\), \(\widetilde D\) as in 1.1.9,
\[ \overline X=X\mathbin{\mathop\times_D}\widetilde D,\qquad X^*=X-X_0=X\mathbin{\mathop\times_D}D^*,\qquad \overline X^*=\overline X-X_0=X\mathbin{\mathop\times_D}\widetilde D^*. \]
We have a commutative diagram
\[ \tag{1.2.7.1}\xymatrix@C=3em@R=3em{ X_0\ar@{^{(}->}[r]^{\bar i}\ar@{=}[d]&\overline X\ar@{_{(}->}[r]^{\bar j}\ar[d]^p&\overline X^*\ar[d]^{p'}\\ X_0\ar@{^{(}->}[r]^i&X\ar@{_{(}->}[r]^j&X^* } \]
cartesian over the diagram

\[ \tag{1.2.7.2}\xymatrix@C=3em@R=3em{ \{0\}\ar@{^{(}->}[r]^{\bar i}\ar@{=}[d]&\widetilde D\ar@{_{(}->}[r]^{\bar j}\ar[d]^p&\widetilde D^*\ar[d]^{p'}\\ \{0\}\ar@{^{(}->}[r]^i&D\ar@{_{(}->}[r]^j&D^*. } \]
For \(F\) a sheaf on \(X\), we set (with the notations of 1.2.4 c))
\[ \Psi(F)=(i^*F,\ \bar i^*\bar j_*((jp')^*F),\ \alpha) \]
where
\[ \alpha:i^*F=\bar i^*p^*F\longrightarrow\bar i^*\bar j_*\bar j^*p^*F \]
is deduced, by application of \(\bar i^*\), from the adjunction arrow \(\operatorname{Id}\to\bar j_*\bar j^*\).
One also defines a functor
\[ \Psi_\eta:(\text{sheaves on }X^*)\longrightarrow(\text{sheaves on }X_0\times\mathscr D^*) \]
by \(\Psi_\eta(F)=\bar i^*\bar j_*(p'^*F)\).
1.2.8. The functoriality properties of \(\Psi\) are the same as those of its analogue studied in XIII 1.3.5 to 1.3.9.

1.3.1. Let \(D\) be a disk, \(0\in D\), \(D^*=D-\{0\}\), \(f:X\to D\) a continuous map and \(X_0=f^{-1}(0)\). The functors \(R\Psi\) and \(R\Psi_\eta\) are the derived functors of the functors \(\Psi\) and \(\Psi_\eta\)
\[ \begin{aligned} R\Psi &:D^+(X)\longrightarrow D^+(X_0\times\mathscr D),\\ R\Psi_\eta &:D^+(X^*)\longrightarrow D^+(X_0\times\mathscr D^*). \end{aligned} \]
We denote by \(R\Phi\) the composite functor \(\Phi\circ R\Psi\) :
\[ R\Phi:D^+(X)\longrightarrow D^+(X_0\times\mathscr D^*). \]

The properties of these functors are all similar to those of the functors of the same name of XIII 2.1. Their proof is much more elementary since, in the topological setting, the base change theorems for a proper morphism or by a smooth morphism \(= localement isomorphe à (\mathbb R^n\times S\to S)\) are easy to prove. Here are the minor changes to be made.
1.3.2. The functor \(Rf_!\) is defined only for a separated morphism between locally compact spaces, and is the derived functor of the direct image with proper supports functor \(f_!\).

1.3.3. The fundamental isomorphism (2.1.8.3), for a proper (and separated) morphism \(f:X\to D\), is to be rewritten as follows. Let \(p:\widetilde D^*\to D^*\) be a universal covering of \(D^*\). For every neighborhood \(U\) of \(0\) in \(D\), let \(\widetilde U^*=p^{-1}(U)\), and \(X_{\widetilde U^*}=X\mathbin{\mathop\times_D}\widetilde U^*\). For \(K\in\operatorname{Ob}D^+(f^{-1}(D^*))\), we have
\[ \tag{1.3.3.1}\varinjlim_U H^i(X_{\widetilde U^*},K)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim H^i(X_0,R\Psi_\eta(K)). \]

This isomorphism is interesting mainly if \(X^*=f^{-1}(D^*)\) is a topological fiber bundle over \(D^*\). For \(U\subset D\) a disk tending to \(0\), the Leray spectral sequence of \(X_{\widetilde U^*}\to\widetilde U^*\) then shows that for every locally constant sheaf \(\Lambda\) on \(X^*\) and for every \(t\in\widetilde U^*\), we have
\[ H^i(X_{\widetilde U^*},\Lambda)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim H^i(X_t,\Lambda). \]
For \(t\in D^*\), we therefore have
\[ \tag{1.3.3.2}H^i(X_t,\Lambda)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim H^i(X_0,R\Psi_t(\Lambda)) \]
(\(X/D\) proper, \(X^*\) a fiber bundle, \(\Lambda\) locally constant).

To verify that \(X^*\) is a fiber bundle over \(D^*\), one uses in practice Thom's isotopy theorem, one form of which is stated below. (For the proof of this theorem, no consequence of which will be used in the following exposés, I refer to Whitney and Mather).

1.3.4. Preliminary : Let \(f:X o Y\) be a smooth (=dfn without critical points) morphism of differentiable manifolds. A connection \( abla\) on \(X/Y\) is a vector subbundle of the tangent bundle of \(X\), complementary to \(\operatorname{Ker}(df)\). For \(u\) a vector field on \(Y\), there exists one and only one vector field \( abla(u)\) on \(X\), lifting \(u\), in this subbundle. We say that \( abla\) is integrable if
\[ \nabla([u,v])=[\nabla(u),\nabla(v)]\ ; \]

for \(f\) proper, an integrable connection defines local trivializations over \(Y\) of \(X/Y\) : \(\alpha:f^{-1}(U)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim X_0\times U\), compatible with the projection onto \(U\), with \(d\alpha(\nabla(u))=(0,u)\). For \(f\) not proper, the same result holds under suitable conditions at infinity, for example if the \(\nabla(u)g\) are zero for \(g\) a suitable exhaustion function.

1.3.5. Thom's isotopy theorem.

Let \(D=\{z\in\mathbb C\mid |z|<1\}\), \(D^*=D-\{0\}\) and \(f:X\to D\) be a proper morphism of analytic spaces. For \(D\) replaced by a smaller disk and \(X\) by its inverse image, there exists a stratification \(\Sigma\) of \(X^*=f^{-1}(D^*)\) having the following properties.

  1. \(a\) \(\Sigma\) is a filtration \(X^*=F_0\supset F_1\supset\cdots\supset F_n\supset F_{n+1}=\varnothing\) of \(X^*\) by closed subsets whose closure \(\overline F_i\) in \(X\) is an analytic subspace of \(X\).
  2. \(b\) The open strata \(F_i-F_{i+1}\) are smooth over \(D^*\).
  3. \(c\) \(\Sigma\) satisfies the conditions \(a\) and \(b\) of Whitney.

For such a stratification \(\Sigma\), there exist on the \(F_i-F_{i+1}\) integrable connections \(\nabla_i\) such that

  1. (i) locally on \(D^*\), \(\nabla_i\) defines a trivialization of \(F_i-F_{i+1}\) ;
  2. (ii) these trivializations glue together to provide local trivializations of \(X^*/D^*\).

Finally, if \((Y_\alpha)\) is a finite family of closed analytic subspaces of \(X\), then, after shrinking \(D\) further if necessary, one can find \(\Sigma\) such that each \(Y_\alpha\) is a union of connected components of open strata \((F_i-F_{i+1})\). A connection \(\nabla\) as above then locally trivializes \((X,(Y_\alpha))\).
1.3.6. We still have (1.3.3.2) for hypercohomology with values in a complex of sheaves \(K\) when \(X\) is proper over \(D\) and the following condition is satisfied

(*) For every \(i\in\mathbb Z\), \(D^*\) is covered by open sets \(U\) such that there exist an isomorphism compatible with the projection onto \(U\), \(\varphi:f^{-1}(U)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim Y\times U\) and a sheaf \(G\) on \(Y\) such that \(\mathcal H^i(K)\) is isomorphic, on \(f^{-1}(U)\), to \(\varphi^*\operatorname{pr}_1^*G\).
Under this hypothesis, the triangle 1.2.1 provides a long exact sequence
\[ \tag{1.3.6.1}\cdots\longrightarrow H^i(X_0,K)\longrightarrow H^i(X_t,K)\longrightarrow H^i(X_0,R\Phi_t(K))\longrightarrow\cdots \]

By Thom's theorem, the condition (*) is satisfied (after shrinking \(D\) if necessary) when there exists a complex analytic stratification of \(X\) such that the \(\mathcal H^i(K)\) are locally constant on each open stratum.
1.4. A particular case.
This section will not be used in the remainder of the seminar. In it, we formalize a construction sketched in the introduction of XIII.
1.4.1. Let \(D\) be an oriented disk, \(0\in D\) and \(D^*=D-\{0\}\). A topological space \(\mathscr X^*\) over \(\mathscr D^*\) is a functor
\[ (\text{universal covers of }D^*)\longrightarrow(\text{topological spaces}). \]

Once a universal covering \(\widetilde D^*\) has been chosen, this is a topological space \(X_\eta\) equipped with an action of \(\pi_1(D^*)\) ; for \(\widetilde D^*\) the universal covering of \((D^*,t)\), one denotes it \(\mathscr X_t^*\). A sheaf on \(\mathscr X^*\) is a functor
\[ (\text{universal covers of }D^*)\longrightarrow(\text{topological spaces equipped with a sheaf}), \]
or equivalently a \(\pi_1(D^*)\)-equivariant sheaf on \(X_\eta\).

To \(\mathscr X^*\), one associates the topological space \(\beta^*\mathscr X^*=X_\eta\times\widetilde D^*/\pi_1(D^*)\) over \(D^*\). Every sheaf \(F\) on \(\mathscr X^*\) defines by passage to the quotient a sheaf \(\beta^*F\) on \(\beta^*\mathscr X^*\), whence a commutative diagram of topoi
\[ \xymatrix@C=5em@R=3em{\beta^*\mathscr X^*\ar[r]\ar[d]&\mathscr X^*\ar[d]\\D^*\ar[r]^\beta&\mathscr D^*.} \]
1.4.2. A topological space \(\mathscr X\) over \(\mathscr D\) consists of

1. \(a\) a topological space \(\mathscr X^*\) over \(\mathscr D^*\) ;
  2. \(b\) a topological space \(X_0\) ;
  3. \(c\) a \(\pi_1(D^*)\)-invariant morphism \(\operatorname{sp}:X_\eta\to X_0\) (\(\pi_1(D^*)\) acting trivially on \(X_0\)).
A sheaf on \(\mathscr X\) consists of a sheaf \(F_0\) on \(X_0\), a sheaf on \(\mathscr X^*\), given by \(F_\eta\), and an equivariant morphism
\[ \operatorname{sp}:\operatorname{sp}^*F_0\longrightarrow F_\eta\quad(\text{or }F_0\longrightarrow\operatorname{sp}_*F_\eta). \]
We have a commutative diagram
\[ \xymatrix@C=3.5em@R=3em{X_0\ar@{^{(}->}[r]\ar[d]&\mathscr X&\mathscr X^*\ar@{_{(}->}[l]\ar[d]\\\{0\}\ar@{^{(}->}[r]&\mathscr D&\mathscr D^*\ar@{_{(}->}[l].} \]

We moreover have a morphism of topoi \(\Psi:\mathscr X\to X_0\times\mathscr D\), by the functoriality of the preceding construction applied to \((X_\eta,X_0,\operatorname{sp})\to(X_0,X_0,\operatorname{Id})\). We have
\[ \Psi(F_0,F_\eta,\operatorname{sp})=(F_0,\operatorname{sp}_*F_\eta,\operatorname{sp}). \]
This morphism induces \(\Psi_\eta:\mathscr X^*\to X_0\times\mathscr D^*\).

1.4.3. To a topological space \(\mathscr X\) over \(\mathscr D\), one associates as follows a topological space \(X=\beta^*\mathscr X\) over \(D\). One adjoins to \(X^*=\beta^*\mathscr X^*\) the fiber \(X_0\) at \(0\), taking as fundamental system of neighborhoods of \(x_0\in X_0\) the
\[ V\cup(\operatorname{sp}^{-1}(V)\times\widetilde U^*/\pi_1(D^*)) \]

for \(V\) a neighborhood of \(x_0\) in \(X_0\), \(U\) a neighborhood of \(0\) in \(D\) and \(\widetilde U^*=U\mathbin{\mathop\times_D}\widetilde D^*\). Let us denote by \(j\) (resp. \(i\)) the inclusion of \(X^*\) (resp. \(X_0\)) into \(X\). For \(F\) a sheaf on \(\mathscr X^*\), we have
\[ i^*j_*\beta^*F=(\operatorname{sp}_*F_\eta)^{\pi_1(D^*)}. \]
From this we obtain a commutative diagram of topoi
\[ \xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{X\ar[r]^\beta\ar[d]&\mathscr X\ar[d]\\D\ar[r]^\beta&\mathscr D} \]
such that, for every sheaf \(F\) on \(\mathscr X\), we have
\[ \tag{1.4.3.1}\Psi(F)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim\Psi(\beta^*F). \]

Proposition 1.4.4.

The arrow (1.4.3.1) derives to give an isomorphism

\[ R\Psi\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim R\Psi\circ\beta^* \]

between functors from \(D^+(\mathscr X)\) into \(D^+(X_0\times\mathscr D)\).

Let \(\bar j\) be the inclusion of \(\overline X^*=X\mathbin{\mathop\times_D}\widetilde D^*\simeq X_\eta\times\widetilde D^*\) into \(\overline X=X\mathbin{\mathop\times_D}\widetilde D\). The proposition reduces to verifying that for every flasque abelian sheaf \(F\) on \(X_\eta\), \(\operatorname{pr}_1^*F\) is acyclic for the functor \(\bar j_*\). This assertion, in turn, reduces to the following one (for \(B=\widetilde D^*\)), well known, which is proved by a homotopy argument.
Let \(F\) be an abelian sheaf on a space \(Y\), and \(B\) a disk. We have
\[ H^i(Y,F)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim H^i(Y\times B,\operatorname{pr}_1^*F). \]

1.4.5. Proposition 1.4.4 provides a procedure for computing \(R\Psi\) for sheaves on \(X\) of the form \(\beta^*F\), essentially reducing the computation of \(R\Psi\) to that of \(R\operatorname{sp}_*\).

1.4.6. We will later have to consider the case where \(X_\eta\) is locally compact and where \(X_0\) is deduced from \(X_\eta\) by contracting to a point \(0\) a compact subset \(V\subset X_\eta\), \(\operatorname{sp}\) being the contraction arrow and the action of \(\pi_1(D^*)\) being trivial outside \(V\). The base change theorem for the proper morphism \(\operatorname{sp}\) then provides, for \(K\in\operatorname{Ob}D^+(\mathscr X^*)\)
\[ \tag{1.4.6.1}R\Psi_\eta(\beta^*K)_0=R\Gamma(V,K) \] \[ \tag{1.4.6.2}R\Gamma_{\{0\}}R\Psi_\eta(\beta^*K)=R\Gamma_V(X_\eta,K) \]
(cohomology with support).

For \(K\) on \(\mathscr X\), with \(\operatorname{sp}:\operatorname{sp}^*K_0\to K_\eta\) a quasi-isomorphism outside \(V\), we have \(R\Phi(K)=0\) outside \(0\). In particular, for a constant sheaf \(\Lambda\) on \(X\), we have
\[ R^i\Phi(\Lambda)=\widetilde H^i(V,\Lambda)\quad\text{concentrated at }0 \]
(reduced cohomology groups).

1.4.7. The case of interest to us is that where \(X_\eta\) is a differentiable manifold and where \(V\subset X_\eta\) is a submanifold with boundary, with interior \(V^\circ\) ; \(X-\{0\}\) is then a differentiable manifold, obtained by gluing \(\beta^*X_\eta\) and the constant bundle over \(D\) with fiber \(X_\eta-V\). The map
\[ \tag{1.4.7.1}H_c^i(V^\circ,\Lambda)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim H_V^i(X_\eta,\Lambda) \]
is an isomorphism. These groups are also denoted \(H^i(V\bmod\partial V,\Lambda)\). The isomorphism (1.4.7.1) defines an isomorphism
\[ \tag{1.4.7.2}R\Gamma_c(V^\circ,\Lambda)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim R\Gamma_{\{0\}}R\Psi_\eta(\Lambda), \]
and the variation is defined (by the procedure of XIII 2.4.5 and via (1.4.7.2)) by a morphism whose source is the reduced cohomology of \(V\)
\[ \tag{1.4.7.3}\operatorname{Var}:R\widetilde\Gamma(V,\Lambda)\longrightarrow R\Gamma_c(V^\circ,\Lambda). \]

\(T\) induces an automorphism, again denoted \(T\), of the pair \((V,\partial V)\) and the restriction \(\partial T\) of \(T\) to \(\partial V\) is the identity. It should be true that the variation 1.4.7.3 coincides with the classical variation morphism (3.1.9) attached to such an automorphism \(T\). A statement of this type will be proved in 3.1.10.
2. The comparison theorem
2.1. For every scheme \(X\) of finite type over \(\mathbb C\), we will denote by \(X_{\mathrm{cl}}\) the space \(X(\mathbb C)\), equipped with its usual topology.

Let \(S\) be a smooth curve over \(\mathbb C\), i.e. a smooth separated scheme of finite type over \(\mathbb C\), purely of dimension 1. Let \(0\in S(\mathbb C)\), \(f:X\to S\) a separated \(S\)-scheme of finite type and \(\Lambda\) a noetherian torsion ring (for example \(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\)). We set \(X_0=f^{-1}(0)\).
2.2. We will denote by \(S^h\) the henselian trait obtained as the henselization of \(S\) at \(0\), and by \(\eta\) the generic point of \(S^h\). We have a functor
\[ \Psi:(\text{sheaves on }X)\xrightarrow{\text{restriction}}(\text{sheaves on }X\times_S S^h)\longrightarrow (\text{sheaves on }X_0\times S^h). \]
The derived functor of \(\Psi\) is the composite
\[ R\Psi:D(X,\Lambda)\longrightarrow D(X\times_S S^h,\Lambda)\xrightarrow{R\Psi}D(X_0\times S^h,\Lambda). \]
2.3. Let \(D\subset S_{\mathrm{cl}}\) be a disk containing \(0\). The composite functor \(\Psi_{\mathrm{cl}}\) (or simply \(\Psi\))

\[ \Psi_{\mathrm{cl}}:(\text{sheaves on }X_{\mathrm{cl}})\xrightarrow{\text{restriction}}(\text{sheaves on }X_{\mathrm{cl}}\times_{S_{\mathrm{cl}}}D)\longrightarrow(\text{sheaves on }(X_0)_{\mathrm{cl}}\times\mathscr D) \]
is independent (up to unique isomorphism) of the choice of \(D\) (recall that \(\mathscr D\) does not change when one replaces \(D\) by a smaller disk). Its derived functor is the composite
\[ R\Psi_{\mathrm{cl}}:D(X_{\mathrm{cl}},\Lambda)\longrightarrow D(f_{\mathrm{cl}}^{-1}(D),\Lambda)\xrightarrow{R\Psi}D(X_0\times\mathscr D,\Lambda). \]
2.4. One likewise defines

\[ \begin{aligned} R\Psi_\eta &:D(X-X_0,\Lambda)\longrightarrow D(X_0\times\eta,\Lambda),\\ R\Psi_{\eta\,\mathrm{cl}} &:D((X-X_0)_{\mathrm{cl}},\Lambda)\longrightarrow D(X_0\times\mathscr D^*,\Lambda). \end{aligned} \]
2.5. The construction 1.1.11 defines a morphism of topoi from \(\mathscr D^*\) into \(\eta\) which extends to
\[ \varepsilon:\mathscr D\longrightarrow S^h. \]
This morphism, and \(\varepsilon:(X_0)_{\mathrm{cl}}\to X_0\) (SGA4 XI 4), define
\[ \varepsilon:(X_0)_{\mathrm{cl}}\times\mathscr D\longrightarrow X_0\times S^h. \]
If \(\widetilde D^*\) is a universal covering of \(D^*\) and \(k(\bar\eta)\) is the corresponding algebraic closure (1.1.10) of \(k(\eta)\), we have
\[ \varepsilon^*(F_0,F_{\bar\eta},\alpha)=(\varepsilon^*F_0,\varepsilon^*F_{\bar\eta},\varepsilon^*\alpha). \]

2.6. Let \(\widetilde D^*\) be a universal covering of \(D^*\), \(k(\bar\eta)\) the corresponding algebraic closure of \(k(\eta)\) and \(\overline S\) the normalization of \(S^h\) in \(k(\bar\eta)\). Let us write \(\overline S\) as a projective limit of affine \(S\)-schemes \(S_i\) ; for each \(i\), and after having replaced \(D\) by a smaller disk \(D_i\), we have a natural morphism of \(S_{\mathrm{cl}}\)-spaces \(\widetilde D_i\xrightarrow{a_i}(S_i)_{\mathrm{cl}}\). Let \(j_i:U_i\hookrightarrow S_i\) be the open subset of \(S_i\), complement of the image \(0\) of the closed point of \(\overline S\), and \(i:0\hookrightarrow S_i\).

Let \(F\) be a sheaf on \(X\). Let \(F_{\mathrm{cl}}=\varepsilon^*F\). Let us again denote by \(j_i\) the inclusion of \(X\times_S U_i\) into \(X\times_S S_i\), by \(j\) that of \(X_0\) into \(X\times_S S_i\), and by \(F\) the inverse image of \(F\) on \(X\times_S S_i\). We have
\[ \Psi(F)=\left(i^*F,\varinjlim_* i^*j_{i*}j_i^*F,\varinjlim(\varphi_i)\right). \]
The functors \(\varepsilon^*\) and inverse image commute. The morphism \(\varepsilon^*j_{i*}\to(j_{i\,\mathrm{cl}})_*\varepsilon^*\) therefore provides
\[ c_i:(i^*j_{i*}j_i^*F)_{\mathrm{cl}}\longrightarrow i^*j_{i*}j_i^*(F_{\mathrm{cl}}). \]

Let \(\bar j\) (resp. \(\bar j_i\)) be the inclusion of \(\widetilde D^*\) (resp. \(\widetilde D_i^*\)) into \(\widetilde D\) (resp. \(\widetilde D_i\)), and the inclusions \(X\times_S\widetilde D^*\hookrightarrow X\times_S\widetilde D\), \(X\times_S\widetilde D_i^*\hookrightarrow X\times_S\widetilde D_i\) deduced from them. The adjunction arrow \(\operatorname{Id}\to a_{i*}a_i^*\) defines
\[ i^*j_{i*}j_i^*F_{\mathrm{cl}}\longrightarrow i^*\bar j_{i*}\bar j_i^*F_{\mathrm{cl}}=i^*\bar j_*\bar j^*F_{\mathrm{cl}}, \]
whence by passage to the limit
\[ c_\eta:\Psi_{\bar\eta}(F)_{\mathrm{cl}}\longrightarrow\Psi_{\mathrm{cl}}(F_{\mathrm{cl}}). \]
This morphism defines the comparison morphism
\[ \tag{2.6.1}c:\varepsilon^*\Psi(F)\longrightarrow\Psi_{\mathrm{cl}}(\varepsilon^*F). \]
One likewise defines
\[ \tag{2.6.2}c_\eta:\varepsilon^*\Psi_\eta(F)\longrightarrow\Psi_{\eta\,\mathrm{cl}}(\varepsilon^*F). \]
2.7. The morphisms 2.6.1 and 2.6.2 derive to give

\[ \begin{aligned} c&:\varepsilon^*R\Psi\longrightarrow R\Psi_{\mathrm{cl}}\circ\varepsilon^*,\\ c_\eta&:\varepsilon^*R\Psi_\eta\longrightarrow R\Psi_{\eta\,\mathrm{cl}}\circ\varepsilon^*. \end{aligned} \]

Theorem 2.8.

Let \(K\in\operatorname{Ob}D(X,\Lambda)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Ob}D(X-X_0,\Lambda)\)) be a complex of sheaves of \(\Lambda\)-modules whose cohomology sheaves \(\mathcal H^i(K)\) are constructible. Then, the comparison morphism

\[ c:\varepsilon^*R\Psi(K)\longrightarrow R\Psi_{\mathrm{cl}}(\varepsilon^*K) \]

(resp. \(c:\varepsilon^*R\Psi_\eta(K)\longrightarrow R\Psi_{\eta\,\mathrm{cl}}(\varepsilon^*K)\)) is an isomorphism.

It suffices to prove the assertion for \(R\Psi_\eta\) ; moreover, the two sides of (2.7) being triangulated functors of finite cohomological dimension, it suffices to treat the case where \(K\) is reduced to a sheaf \(F\) (placed in degree 0). The proof is entirely similar to that of the finiteness theorem XIII 2.3.1 : since \(R\Psi\) and \(R\Psi_{\mathrm{cl}}\) have the same variance properties, one reduces by the dévissages of loc. cit. (where one allows oneself to shrink \(S\)) to the case where \(X\) is regular, \(f^{-1}(0)\) a divisor with normal crossings in \(X\) and where \(F\) is \(\mathbb Z/\ell\). One then compares I 3.3 with its transcendental analogue.
3. Isolated singularities
3.1. The variety of vanishing cycles.

3.1.1. Let \(D=\{z\in\mathbb C\mid |z|<1\}\), \(X\) an analytic space, \(0\in X\) and \(f:X\to D\) a morphism such that \(f(0)=0\). We assume that \(f\) is smooth outside \(0\) (in particular, \(X\) is smooth outside \(0\)). We propose to study the vanishing cycles in a neighborhood of \(0\) ; we therefore do not restrict the generality by assuming \(X\) embedded in \(\mathbb C^n\times D\subset\mathbb C^{n+1}\), in a way compatible with the projection onto \(D\), and so that \(0\) is sent to \(0\).
3.1.2. Let \(\delta(x)\) be a real analytic function on \(X\) (the restriction to \(X\) of a real analytic function on \(\mathbb C^{n+1}\)), such that, for \(x\in X_0=f^{-1}(0)\), we have

1. \(a\) \(\delta(x)\geq0\) ;
  2. \(b\) \(\delta(x)=0\Longleftrightarrow x=0\).

One will take for example for \(\delta\) the square of the Euclidean distance, in \(\mathbb C^{n+1}\), from \(x\) to \(0\). The locus of critical points of the restriction of \(\delta\) to \(X_0\) is real analytic, hence, in a suitable neighborhood of \(0\), has only a finite number of connected components. Replacing \(X\) by an open neighborhood of \(0\) and applying Sard, one can therefore obtain the following situation

1. \(c\) for suitable \(r>0\), \(X_0\cap\delta^{-1}([0,r])\) is compact,
  2. \(d\) on \(X_0\), \(\delta\) has no critical value \(\leq r\).
After shrinking \(X\) further if necessary, we then have

1. \(e\) there exists a function \(C^\infty\) \(\tau(s)\) (\(0\leq s\leq r\)), with \(\tau(0)=0\) and \(\tau'(s)>0\) for \(s>0\), such that, for \(0<s\leq r\) and \(0\leq |t|^2\leq\tau(s)\), \(X_t=f^{-1}(t)\) is transverse to the real hypersurface \(\delta(x)=s\) of \(X\) and the box \[ B_{t,d}=\{x\in X\mid\delta(x)\leq d\text{ and }|f(x)|^2<t\} \] is proper over the open disk \(D_t\) of radius \(t^{1/2}\).

Let \(L=X_0\cap\delta^{-1}(r)\). By \(c\) and \(d\), there exists a homeomorphism of \(X_0\cap\delta^{-1}([0,r])\) with the cone \((L\times[0,r]/\text{contraction of }L\times\{0\}\text{ to a point})\), transforming \(\delta\) into the 2nd coordinate, and \(C^\infty\) outside the origin.
3.1.3. Let \(d\leq r\) and \(t\leq\tau(d)\). Outside \(0\), \(B_{t,d}\) is a manifold with corners and the projection \(f\) of \(B_{t,d}\) onto \(D_t\) a fibration. In particular

1. \(a\) \(B_{t,d}\) induces on \(D_t^*=D_t-\{0\}\) a fiber bundle \(C^\infty\) in compact manifolds with boundary ;
  2. \(b\) the boundary \(\delta(x)=d\) of this bundle is extended to a fiber bundle \(C^\infty\) \(\partial B_{t,d}\) in compact manifolds over \(D_t\).

Let \(y\in D_t^*\). We will call the manifold with boundary \(V=f^{-1}(y)\) the variety of vanishing cycles. The disk being contractible, the bundle \(\partial B_{t,d}\) over \(D_t\) is isomorphic to the constant bundle with fiber \(\partial V\), and the set of bundle isomorphisms \[\partial B_{t,d}\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim\partial V\times D_t,\] which are the identity on the fiber at \(y\), is contractible. Let us choose one, denoted \(\alpha\). Let us lift the vector field \(\frac\partial{\partial\theta}\) on \(S_{|y|}\) (circle of radius \(|y|\)) to a vector field \(u\) on \(f^{-1}(S_{|y|})\) which, on the boundary, is compatible with \(\alpha\) (\(d\alpha(u)=(\frac\partial{\partial\theta},0)\)). The set of these liftings is contractible. By integration (going around once in the positive direction), \(u\) defines a diffeomorphism \(T\) of \(V\) onto itself, which is the identity on \(\partial V\) ; this is the monodromy transformation. It is well defined modulo isotopies mod \(\partial V\).

In a sense which we leave to the reader the care of making precise, \((V,T)\) is independent of the choices of \(t\) and \(s\). I do not know whether it is independent of the choice of \(\delta\). One verifies however that this is so when one restricts to taking for \(\delta\) the restriction to \(X\) of a function \(g\) on \(\mathbb C^{n+1}\) such that, on the Zariski tangent space of \(X_0\) at \(0\), we have \(dg=0\) and \(d^2g>0\) (positive definite). The point is that, for \(\delta\) and \(\delta'\) of this type, one can choose \(r\) as in 3.1.2 \(c\) \(d\) uniformly for all the \(\lambda\delta+(1-\lambda)\delta'\) \((0\leq\lambda\leq1)\).

3.1.4. Let \((V,T)\) and \((V_1,T_1)\) be deduced from \((V,T)\) by adjoining a collar : \[V_1-V^\circ\simeq\partial V\times[0,1]\quad\text{and}\quad T_1\text{ is the identity on }V_1-V^\circ.\] Let \(f':W\to D_t\) be the following space over \(D_t\) (cf. 1.4.3)

1. \(a\) Let \(\widetilde D_t^*\) be a universal covering of \(D_t^*\) with base point \(y\) and \(W^*\) the bundle over \(D_t^*\) quotient of \(V_1\times\widetilde D_t^*\) by the relation generated by \((v,y)\simeq(T_1v,e^{2\pi i}y)\).
  2. \(b\) Since \(T_1|V_1-V^\circ\) is trivial, one can glue \(W^*\) and the trivial bundle over \(D_t\) with fiber \(V_1-V\) into a space \(W_1\) over \(D_t\).
  3. \(c\) \(W=W_1\cup\{0\}\), with \(f'(0)=0\). \(W\) is equipped with the unique topology inducing that of \(W_1\), for which \(f'\) is continuous and \(f'^{-1}(\overline D_{t'})\) \((t'<t)\) is the one-point compactification of \(W_1\cap f'^{-1}(\overline D_{t'})\).
One equips \(W-\{0\}\) with the integrable connection (1.3.4) for which the trivialization provided by \(a\) of the inverse image of \(W^*\) on \(\widetilde D^*\) is horizontal.

Proposition 3.1.5.

There exists a homeomorphism \(\Psi\), compatible with the projection onto \(D_t\) and sending \(0\) to \(0\), of \(B_{s,t}\) with \(W\) ; this homeomorphism can be chosen to be a diffeomorphism outside \(0\).
This result will not be used in the remainder of the seminar.

Let \(d_0<d_1\leq r\), \(t_0\leq\tau(d_0)\) and \(y\in D_{t_0}^*\). We have \(B_{t_0,d_0}\subset B_{t_0,d_1}\) and \(B_{t_0,d_1}-B_{t_0,d_0}^\circ\) is a fiber bundle over \(D_{|t|_0}\). Let us trivialize it and, above the circle \(S_{|y|}\), lift the vector field \(\frac\partial{\partial\theta}\) in a way compatible with this trivialization. Let \(T_1\) be the corresponding endomorphism of \(V_1=f^{-1}(y)\cap B_{t_0,d_1}\), and \(V_0=f^{-1}(y)\cap B_{t_0,d_0}\). Then, \((V_0\subset V_1,T_1)\) is isomorphic to \((V\subset V_1,T_1)\) of 3.1.4. Let \(W\) be constructed by means of \((V_0\subset V_1,T_1)\). We have \(f'^{-1}(y)=V_1\), canonically. The proof will show that one can take \(\Psi\) inducing the identity \(f^{-1}(y)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim f'^{-1}(y)\).
The following pictures (\(f\), and its restriction to \(f^{-1}(\mathbb R)\)) summarize the proof.
[Diagrams of \(f:B_{t_0,d_1}\to D_t\) and of its restriction to \(f^{-1}(\mathbb R)\)]

Let \(B^+\subset B_{t_0,d_1}\) be the set of \(x\) such that \(|f(x)|^2\leq\tau(\delta(x))\). Let \(\varphi_n\) be a sequence of functions \(C^\infty\) from \([0,t_0]\) into \((0,d_1]\) such that, for a sequence \(r_0>r_1>\cdots\) tending to \(0\), we have

1. \(a\) for \(|t|^2\geq r_i\), \(\varphi_i\) is the inverse of the function \(\tau\) ;
  2. \(b\) for \(|t|^2<r_i\) and \(j>i\), \(\varphi_j(t)<\varphi_i(t)\).

The set \(B^+-\{0\}\) is a union of the bundles over \(D_t\), \(B_i^+\) : \(\delta(x)\leq\varphi_i(|f(x)|^2)\). A standard argument, using the fact that \(\delta\) has no critical points on the fibers of \(B^+-\{0\}\to D_t\), shows that there exists a diffeomorphism \(\alpha_i\) of \(B_i^+\) with \(B_{i+1}^+\), compatible with the projection onto \(D_t\), and the identity outside a small neighborhood of the subspace \[(\delta(x)=\varphi_i(|f(x)|^2),\quad |f(x)|^2\leq r_i)\quad\text{of }B_i^+.\]

Composing the \(\alpha_i\) and passing to the limit, one obtains a diffeomorphism \(\alpha\) of \(B_0^+-\{x\mid x=0\text{ and }\delta(x)=\varphi_0(0)\}\) with \(B^+-\{0\}\). Let us transport by \(\alpha\) a trivialization of the bundle \(B_0^+\) over \(D_t\) to obtain an integrable connection \(\nabla'\) on \(B^+-\{0\}\). One can then

1. \(a\) extend the restriction of this connection to \(f^{-1}(S_{|y|})\cap B^+\) to a connection on \(f^{-1}(S_{|y|})/S_{|y|}\) (this extension giving \(T_1\)) ;
  2. \(b\) extend to \(B_{t_0,d_1}\) the lifting on \(B^+-\{0\}\) provided by \(\nabla'\) of the radial vector field on \(D_t\).

There then exist one and only one integrable connection \(\nabla\) on \(B_{t_0,d_1}\) which extends \(\nabla'\), \(a\) and \(b\), and one and only one horizontal diffeomorphism of \(B_{t_0,d_1}-\{0\}\) with \(W-\{0\}\) which is the identity on \(f^{-1}(y)\). It answers the problem.

3.1.6. A generalization. The foregoing generalizes to the case where \(f:X\to D\) is a morphism such that \(0\in X\) is an isolated point of non-smoothness in \(X_0\). The locus of non-smoothness of \(f\) is then finite over \(D\) (after shrinking \(X\) and \(D\) if necessary). The variety of vanishing cycles \(V\) then has a finite set \(\Sigma\) of critical points ; \(V-\Sigma\) is a differentiable manifold with boundary (with compact boundary, but not compact). The monodromy transformation \(T\) is the identity on the boundary and leaves \(\Sigma\) stable. Finally, 3.1.5 remains valid, the fibered homeomorphism \(\Psi\) being a diffeomorphism outside the critical locus, which it sends to what in \(W\) comes from \(\Sigma\).
3.1.7. Proposition 3.1.5 makes it possible to use the computations of 1.4. One thus finds that

1. \(A\) The groups of vanishing cycles \((R^i\Phi(\Lambda))_0\) are the reduced cohomology groups, with values in \(\Lambda\), of the variety of vanishing cycles \(V\).
  2. \(B\) The groups \(H_{\{0\}}^i(X_0,R\Psi(\Lambda))\) are the cohomology groups with proper support of \(V^\circ\), with values in \(\Lambda\).
  3. \(C\) The variation \[\operatorname{Var}:\widetilde H^i(V,\Lambda)\longrightarrow H_c^i(V^\circ,\Lambda)=H^i(V\bmod\partial V,\Lambda)\] is the classical variation, defined by the automorphism \(T\) of \(V\) (which is the identity on \(\partial V\)).

3.1.8. The assertions \(A\) \(B\) \(C\) are in fact more elementary than 3.1.5 ; here is a direct proof-construction of them, which will spare us verifying that the isomorphisms \(A\) \(B\) are independent of the choices made in the construction 3.1.5.
Let \(d\leq r\), \(t\leq\tau(d)\) and \(B\) be the open box
\[B=\{x\mid\delta(x)<d\quad\text{and}\quad |f(x)|^2<t\}.\]
Let \(\widetilde D^*\) be a universal covering of \(D^*\), and
\[ \tag{3.1.8.1} \xymatrix{\{0\}\ar@{=}[d]\ar[r]^{\bar i}&\widetilde D_t\ar[d]^p&\widetilde D_t^*\ar[l]_{\bar j}\ar[d]^{p'}\\ \{0\}\ar[r]^i&D_t&D_t^*\ar[l]_j} \]
deduced from 1.2.7.2 by restriction to \(D_t\). Let
\[ \tag{3.1.8.2} \xymatrix{B_0\ar@{=}[d]\ar[r]^{\bar i}&\overline B\ar[d]^p&\overline B^*\ar[l]_{\bar j}\ar[d]^{p'}\\ B_0\ar[r]^i&B&B^*\ar[l]_j} \]
be the inverse image of \(f:B\to D\) on (3.1.8.1). We again denote by \(f\) the projection of (3.1.8.2) onto (3.1.8.1).

\(A\) By definition, \(R^i\Psi_\eta(\Lambda)_0\) is the inductive limit, for \(U\) running through the neighborhoods of \(0\) in \(X\), of the cohomology groups \(H^i(U\times_D\widetilde D^*,\Lambda)\). Let us make \(U\) run through the fundamental system of \(0\) formed of the boxes \(B\) as above. The fibered product \(U\times_D\widetilde D^*=\overline B^*\) is a bundle with fiber \(V^\circ\) over \(\widetilde D_t^*\) (contractible). The inductive system of the \(H^i(\overline B^*,\Lambda)\) is therefore constant, with value \(H^i(V^\circ,\Lambda)=H^i(V,\Lambda)\).
The isomorphisms obtained
\[ \tag{3.1.8.3}R^i\Psi_\eta(\Lambda)_0=H^i(V,\Lambda) \]
can be made precise as a composite isomorphism (cf. \(B\) below).

\[ \tag{3.1.8.4} \xymatrix{ R\Psi_\eta(\Lambda)_0 & RI(B_0,R\Psi_\eta(\Lambda))\ar[l]_-\sim & (Rf_*R\bar j_*\Lambda)_0\ar[l]_-\sim & R\Gamma(\widetilde D_t,Rf_*R\bar j_*\Lambda)_0\ar[l]_-\sim\ar@{=}[d]\\ &R\Gamma(V,\Lambda)\ar[r]^-\sim&R\Gamma(V^\circ,\Lambda)&R\Gamma(\overline B^*,\Lambda)\ar[l]_-\sim } \]
The complex \(R\Phi(\Lambda)_0\) is defined by the distinguished triangle
\[ \Lambda\longrightarrow(R\Psi_\eta\Lambda)_0\longrightarrow R\Phi(\Lambda)_0\longrightarrow. \]
The \(R^i\Phi(\Lambda)_0\) are therefore the reduced cohomology groups of \(V\).
\(B\) Let \(\Phi\) be the set of closed subsets of \(\overline B^*\) whose closure in \(\overline B\) is proper over \(\widetilde D\). The following arrows are isomorphisms

\[ \tag{3.1.8.5} \xymatrix{ R\Gamma_{\{0\}}(R\Psi_\eta(\Lambda))\ar[r]^-{\overset{\sim}{\scriptscriptstyle 1}}& R\Gamma_c(B_0,R\Psi_\eta(\Lambda))& (Rf_!R\bar j_*\Lambda)_0\ar[l]_-{\overset{\sim}{\scriptscriptstyle 2}}\\ R\Gamma_c(V^\circ,\Lambda)&R\Gamma_\Phi(\overline B^*,\Lambda)\ar[l]_-{\overset{\sim}{\scriptscriptstyle 5}}& R\Gamma(\widetilde D_t,Rf_!R\bar j_*\Lambda)\ar[l]_-{\overset{\sim}{\scriptscriptstyle 4}}\ar[u]_-{\overset{\sim}{\scriptscriptstyle 3}} } \]

1. ① because \(B_0\) is a cone (3.1.2) and \(R^i\Psi_\eta(\Lambda)\) is constant outside (0) ;
  2. ② by the base change theorem for direct images with proper support ;
  3. ③ because \(Rf_!R\bar j_*\Lambda\) is constant outside (0),
  4. ④ is the « derived category » version of the spectral sequence of a composite functor ;
  5. ⑤ because \(\widetilde D^*\) is contractible).
The isomorphism (3.1.8.5) provides the desired isomorphisms
\[ \tag{3.1.8.6}R^i\Gamma_{\{0\}}(R\Psi_\eta(\Lambda))=H_c^i(V^\circ,\Lambda). \]

3.1.9. (cf. [3]) Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(i:Y\hookrightarrow X\) a closed subset of \(X\), \(T\) an automorphism of \(X\), inducing the identity on \(Y\), and \(\Lambda\) an abelian group. To define the variation \(\operatorname{Var}\), one chooses a \(T\)-equivariant resolution \(\Lambda_X^*\) of \(\Lambda\) on \(X\), a resolution \(\Lambda_Y^*\) of the sheaf \(\Lambda\) on \(Y\), on which \(T\) acts trivially by convention, and an equivariant morphism
\[ \varphi:\Lambda_X^*\longrightarrow i_*\Lambda_Y^*, \]

which resolves the obvious morphism \(\varphi_0:\Lambda_X\to i_*\Lambda_Y\). One assumes the sheaves \(\Lambda_X^i\) and \(\Lambda_Y^i\) acyclic for the functors that will be considered. Passing to global sections, one finds
\[ \Gamma(\varphi):\Gamma(\Lambda_X^*)\longrightarrow\Gamma(\Lambda_Y^*). \]
Let us assume for simplicity that the \(\Gamma(\varphi)^i\) are surjective (otherwise, one makes them surjective by adding to \(\Gamma(\Lambda_X^*)\) the cone of \(\Gamma(\Lambda_Y^*)\)). We set
\[ R\Gamma(X\bmod Y,\Lambda)=\ker(\Gamma(\varphi)). \]
Under reasonable hypotheses, if \(\Phi\) is the set of closed subsets of \(X\) disjoint from \(Y\), we have
\[ \Gamma_\Phi(X-Y,\Lambda_X^*)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim R\Gamma(X\bmod Y,\Lambda) \]

(quasi-isomorphism). The endomorphism (T-1) of \(\Gamma(\Lambda_X^*)\) (\(T\) acts by transport of structure) is zero on \(\Lambda\) (the constants) and factors through \(\ker(\Gamma(\varphi))\), whence, in the derived category and under the same hypotheses, a variation morphism
\[ \operatorname{Var}:R\widetilde\Gamma(X,\Lambda)\longrightarrow R\Gamma_\Phi(X-Y,\Lambda) \]
(without hypothesis, one finds \(\operatorname{Var}:R\widetilde\Gamma(X,\Lambda)\to R\Gamma(X\bmod Y,\Lambda)\)).

This description simplifies if \(T\) is the identity in a neighborhood of \(Y\) and, in this neighborhood, is the identity on \(\Lambda_X^*\). The endomorphism (T-1) of \(\Gamma(\Lambda_X^*)\) then factors through \(\Gamma_\Phi(\Lambda_X^*)\), and provides the variation.

If conversely \(f_\alpha\) is a section of unipotent finite determination, \(f_{\alpha,k}\) defined by (4.7.3) is zero for \(k\) large (since \(N^kf_\alpha=0\) for \(k\) large) and is invariant under \(T\), hence is identified with a section of \(F\) on \(X^*\). Let us show, which comes to the same thing, that \(Nf_{\alpha,k}=0\) : one applies to \(N^kf_\alpha\) the identity
\[ N\sum_{j\geq0}(-1)^j\frac{(\log z)^j}{j!}N^j =\sum_{j>0}(-1)^j\frac{(\log z)^{j-1}}{(j-1)!}N^j +\sum_{j\geq0}(-1)^j\frac{(\log z)^j}{j!}N^{j+1}=0. \]
Finally we have

\[ \sum_{k\geq0}f_{\alpha,k}\frac{(\log z)^k}{k!} =\sum_{j,k\geq0}(-1)^j\frac{(\log z)^{j+k}}{j!\,k!}N^{j+k}f_\alpha =\sum_{n\geq0}\left(\sum_j(-1)^j\binom nj\right)\frac{(\log z)^n}{n!}N^nf_\alpha=f_\alpha, \]
which proves 4.7.
Remark 4.8.1. The condition \(\Re(\alpha)\in[0,1[\) of 4.7 can be replaced by \(\alpha\in C\), for \(C\) any fundamental domain for \(\mathbb C\to\mathbb C/\mathbb Z\).

Remark 4.8.2. Let \(f\) be of finite determination, with its decomposition (4.7.1). For \(0\leq\Re(\alpha)<1\), and \(\alpha\) such that some \(f_{\alpha,k}\neq0\), let \(m(\alpha)=1+\sup\{k\mid f_{\alpha,k}\neq0\}\). If all the \(f_{\alpha,k}\) are zero, let us set \(m(\alpha)=0\). The characteristic polynomial of \(T\) acting on \(V(f)\) is
\[P_c(T,V(f))=\prod_\alpha(X-\exp(2\pi i\alpha))^{m(\alpha)}.\]
In particular, \(f\) is of quasi-unipotent finite determination if and only if in (4.7.1) one can restrict \(\alpha\) to be in \(\mathbb Q\).
4.9. Let us return to the more restrictive hypotheses of 4.1 and let \(F\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\) whose restriction to \(X^*\) is locally free.

Proposition 4.10.

Let \(f\) be a section of finite determination of the inverse image \(\overline F\) of \(F\) on \(\overline X^*\). In order that \(f\) be of moderate growth along \(X_0\), it is necessary and sufficient that the \(f_{\alpha,k}\) of the decomposition (4.7.1) be meromorphic along \(X_0\).

For the definition of « of moderate growth », I refer to [1] 2.10. It is clear from (4.7.1), (4.7.2), (4.7.3) that \(f\) is of moderate growth if and only if the \(f_{\alpha,k}\) are ; for sections of \(F\) on \(X^*\), we know that « of moderate growth along \(X_0\) » is equivalent to « meromorphic along \(X_0\) ».
For \(X=D\) and \(F=\mathcal O\), 4.10 has the following elementary corollary.

Corollary 4.11.

Let \(K\) be the field of germs at 0 of meromorphic functions on \(D\), and \(K^m\) (resp. \(K^{mu}\), \(K^{mqu}\)) the \(K\)-algebra of germs at 0 of holomorphic functions on \(\widetilde D^*\), of moderate growth at 0 and of finite determination (resp. of unipotent finite determination, resp. quasi-unipotent). Then, \(K^m\), \(K^{mu}\), \(K^{mqu}\) admit over \(K\) the following bases

\[ \begin{array}{lll} K^m&\text{the }z^\alpha(\log z)^k&(0\leq\Re(\alpha)<1,\ k\in\mathbb N),\\ K^{mu}&\text{the }(\log z)^k,&\\ K^{mqu}&\text{the }z^\alpha(\log z)^k&(\alpha\in\mathbb Q,\ 0\leq\alpha<1,\ k\in\mathbb N). \end{array} \]

Under the hypotheses of 4.9, let us denote by \(\Psi_\eta^m(F)\) (resp. \(\Psi_\eta^{mu}(F)\), resp. \(\Psi_\eta^{mqu}(F)\)) the subsheaf of \(\bar i^*\bar j_*(\overline F)\) formed of the sections of \(\overline F\) in a neighborhood of the points of \(X_0\) which are of moderate growth along \(X_0\) and of finite determination (resp. unipotent finite, resp. quasi-unipotent finite). Let \(F^m\subset j_*j^*F\) be the sheaf on \(X\) of sections of \(j_*j^*F\) meromorphic along \(X_0\). Proposition 4.10 has the following corollary, independent of the arbitrary choices of \(z\) and \(\log z\).

Corollary 4.12.

The natural morphisms

\[ \begin{array}{rcl} \text{(i)}&K^m\otimes_K i^*F^m&\longrightarrow\Psi_\eta^m(F),\\ \text{(ii)}&K^{mu}\otimes_K i^*F^m&\longrightarrow\Psi_\eta^{mu}(F),\\ \text{(iii)}&K^{mqu}\otimes_K i^*F^m&\longrightarrow\Psi_\eta^{mqu}(F) \end{array} \]

are isomorphisms.
The main result of this paragraph is the following.

Theorem 4.13.

The natural morphism, induced by 4.3.1,

\[ \tag{4.13.1} \Psi_\eta^{mqu}(\Omega_X^*)\longrightarrow R\Psi_\eta(\mathbb C) \]

is an isomorphism in \(D^+(X_0,\mathbb C)\).
We will in fact prove more general results (4.15) (4.17.) whose statement requires some preliminaries which the reader interested only in 4.13 may dispense with reading.

4.14. Let \(V\) be a locally constant sheaf of finite-dimensional \(\mathbb C\)-vector spaces on \(X^*\) (a local system). Let \(\mathcal V=V\otimes_\mathbb C\mathcal O\) be the corresponding locally free \(\mathcal O\)-module and \(\nabla\) the integrable connection on \(\mathcal V\) for which the local sections of \(V\) are horizontal. We know that \(\mathcal V\) admits an extension \(\widetilde{\mathcal V}\) to a coherent sheaf on \(X\), such that the connection \(\nabla\) is regular along \(X_0\) relative to this extension. We also know that two such extensions are meromorphically equivalent ([1] II 5.7) ; the sheaf \(\Psi_\eta^m(\widetilde{\mathcal V})\), (and its variants \(\Psi^{mu}\), \(\Psi^{mqu}\)), therefore does not depend on the \(\mathcal V\) chosen ; we denote it \(\Psi_\eta^m(\mathcal V)\). One likewise defines \(\Psi_\eta^m(\Omega_{X^*}^*(V))=\Psi_\eta^m(\Omega_X^*\otimes\mathcal V)\), and its variants \(\Psi^{mu}\), \(\Psi^{mqu}\).

Proposition 4.15.

The natural morphism, induced by (4.4.1)

\[ \tag{4.15.1} \Psi_\eta^m(\Omega_{X^*}^*(V))\longrightarrow R\Psi_\eta(V) \]

is an isomorphism in \(D^+(X_0,\mathbb C)\).

4.16. One says that \(V\) as in 4.14 is unipotent (resp. quasi-unipotent) along \(X_0\) if, for every morphism \(u\) from the standard disk \(D_0\) into \(X\), with \(u^{-1}(X_0)=\{0\}\), the monodromy transformation of the local system \(u^*V\) on \(D_0^*\) is unipotent (resp. quasi-unipotent).

Proposition 4.17.

If \(V\) is quasi-unipotent, the natural morphism induced by (4.4.1)

\[ \tag{4.17.1} \Psi_\eta^{mqu}(\Omega_{X^*}^*(V))\longrightarrow R\Psi_\eta(V) \]

is an isomorphism in \(D^+(X_0,\mathbb C)\).
Theorem 4.13 is the particular case of 4.17 where \(V=\mathbb C\).

4.18. We will first treat the case where \(X\) is smooth and where \((X_0)_{\mathrm{red}}\) is a divisor with normal crossings in \(X\). The theorems being local, we may assume that \(f\) is the map \(f(z)=\prod_1^d z_i^{n_i}\), \((0<d\leq e,\ n_i>0)\) from \(X=D^e\) into \(D\), where \(D\) this time denotes the standard disk \(D=\{z\in\mathbb C\mid |z|<1\}\), and it suffices to prove that the stalk at \(0\in D^e\) of (4.15.1) (resp. 4.17.1) is a quasi-isomorphism.

Let \(U\) be a neighborhood of 0 in \(D^e\) of the form \(|z_i|<a\) \((0<a\leq1)\). One defines \(U_0\), \(\overline U\), \(U^*\), \(\overline U^*\) by taking the inverse image of \(U\) in 4.1.1. The cohomology of \(U^*\) or \(\overline U^*\) is independent of \(a\), so that
\[H^*(\overline U^*,V)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim(R^*\Psi_\eta(V))_0.\]

The fundamental group \(\mathbb Z^d\) of \(X^*=(D^*)^d\times D^{e-d}\) (equal to that of \(U^*\)) being abelian, \(V\) admits a finite filtration whose successive quotients are local systems of rank one. By dévissage, we may therefore assume \(V\) of rank one.

For \(\widetilde{\mathcal V}\) as in 4.14, we will denote by \(j_*^m\Omega_{X^*}^*(V)\) the subcomplex of \(j_*\Omega_{X^*}^*(V)\) with components the \((\Omega_X^p\otimes\widetilde{\mathcal V})^m\). By [1] II 6.4.1 and 6.8, the natural map deduced from the quasi-isomorphism \(V\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim\Omega_{X^*}^*(V)\) (holomorphic Poincaré lemma),
\[ \tag{4.18.1} j_*^m\Omega_{X^*}^*(V)\longrightarrow Rj_*V \]
is a quasi-isomorphism. We have
\[ \tag{4.18.2} H^*((j_*^m\Omega_{X^*}^*(V))_0)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim(R^*j_*V)_0 \mathop{\longleftarrow}\limits^\sim H^*(U,V). \]
This will be the only analytic ingredient of this part 4.18 of the proof.

Lemma 4.18.3.

For \(V\) of rank one, we have

(i) for \(V\) nontrivial : \(H^*(U^*,V)=0\)

(ii) for \(V=\mathbb C\) : \(H^*(U^*,V)=\bigwedge^*\mathbb C^d\).
Indeed, \(U^*\) has the homotopy type of a torus.
Let \(\eta\) be the generator of \(H^1(D^*,\mathbb Z)\), and its inverse image in \(H^1(U^*,\mathbb Z)\).

Lemma 4.18.4.

For \(V\) of rank one, the natural map

\[ H^*(U^*,\mathbb C)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim_{(4.18.1)}H^*((j_*^m\Omega_{X^*}^*(V))_0) \longrightarrow H^*(\Psi_\eta^{mu}(\Omega_{X^*}^*(V))_0) \]

identifies \(H^*(\Psi_\eta^{mu}(\Omega_{X^*}^*(V))_0)\) with \(H^*(U^*,V)/\eta H^*(U^*,V)\).

Let us use 4.12 (ii), and the identity \(d\log f=\sum n_i\dfrac{dz_i}{z_i}\). The complex \(\Psi_\eta^{mu}(\Omega_{X^*}^*(V))_0\) is identified with the simple complex \(\underline sK\) associated (by taking sums) with the double complex \(K\) with components
\[K^{-p,n+p}=\frac{(\log f)^p}{p!}\cdot(j_*^m\Omega_{X^*}^n(V))_0.\]

This complex is in a bad quadrant (\(p\leq0\), \(q\geq0\), \(p+q\geq0\)) ; however, \(\underline sK\) is the inductive limit of the subcomplexes \(\underline s\,\sigma_{p\geq p_0}(K)\) with components
\[(\underline s\,\sigma_{p\geq p_0}K)^n=\bigoplus_{\substack{p+q=n\\p\geq p_0}}K^{pq},\]
and this suffices to justify the argument below.
The spectral sequence of the filtration by the first degree is written
with \(d_1=2\pi i\eta\wedge:H^n(U^*,V)\longrightarrow H^{n+1}(U^*,V)\).

If \(V\) is nontrivial, \(E_1=0\) by 4.18.3 and 4.18.4 reduces to \(0=0\). Suppose that \(V=\mathbb C\). The \((E_1,d_1)\) are then Koszul complexes, possibly truncated, so that (acyclicity of the Koszul complex)
\[ E_2^{pq}=0\quad\text{for }p\neq0, \qquad E_2^{0n}=H^n(U^*,V)/\eta H^{n-1}(U^*,V) \]
which verifies 4.18.4.

For \(\beta\in\mathbb C^*\), let us denote by \(V_\beta\) the local system of rank one on \(D^*\), with trivialized inverse image on \(\widetilde D^*\), and with monodromy \(\beta\). Let us again denote by \(V_\beta\) its inverse image on \(U^*\). On \(\overline U^*\), the inverse image of \(V\otimes V_\beta\) is canonically isomorphic to \(V\).

Lemma 4.18.5.

For \(V\) of rank one, the map

\[ \bigoplus_{\beta\in\mathbb C^*}H^*(U^*,V\otimes V_\beta)/\eta H^*(U^*,V\otimes V_\beta) \longrightarrow H^*(\overline U^*,V) \]

is bijective.

Let \(k\) be the gcd of the \(n_i\). The space \(\overline U^*\) has \(k\) connected components, parametrized by the \(k^{\text{th}}\) roots of unity ; on the one, \(\overline U^*_\xi\), of index \(\xi\), the inverse image of the function \(z^{1/k}\) on \(\widetilde D^*\) coincides with the inverse image of the function \(\xi:\prod_1^d z_i^{n_i/k}\) on \(U^*\). The component \(\overline U^*_\xi\) has the homotopy type of a torus, and the sequence
\[ 0\longrightarrow\pi_1(\overline U^*_\xi)\longrightarrow\pi_1(U^*)\longrightarrow\pi_1(D^*) \]

is exact. Let us denote by \(\varphi:\mathbb Z^d\to\mathbb Z\) the map \(\pi_1(U^*)\to\pi_1(D^*)\). If \(\operatorname{Ker}(\varphi)\) acts nontrivially on \(V\), the two sides of 4.18.4 are zero. Otherwise, replacing \(V\) by a \(V\otimes V_\beta\), we may assume that \(V\) is trivial, say \(V=\mathbb C\). We then have in complex cohomology
\[ H^0(\overline U^*)\otimes H^*(U^*)/\eta H^*(U^*)\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim H^*(\overline U^*) \]
and one concludes by noting that \(V_\beta\) is trivial on \(U^*\) if \(\beta^k=1\), that
\[ \bigoplus_{\beta^k=1}H^0(U^*,V_\beta)=\bigoplus_{\beta^k=1}\mathbb C \mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim H^0(\overline U^*,\mathbb C), \]
and that, for \(\beta^k\neq1\), \(H^*(U^*,V_\beta)=0\).
Remark 4.18.6. If \(V\) is quasi-unipotent, one can in 4.18.5 replace the summation over \(\mathbb C^*\) by a summation over the roots of unity : the proof is the same.
Applying 4.12, one finds a commutative diagram

\[ \begin{array}{ccccc} H^*\Psi_\eta^m(\Omega_{X^*}^*(V))_0&\mathop{\longleftarrow}\limits^\sim_{4.12}&\displaystyle\bigoplus_\beta H^*\Psi_\eta^{mu}(\Omega_{X^*}^*(V\otimes V_\beta))_0&\mathop{\longleftarrow}\limits^\sim_{4.18.4}&\displaystyle\bigoplus_\beta H^*(U^*,V\otimes V_\beta)/\eta H^*(U^*,V\otimes V_\beta)\\ \downarrow&&&&\downarrow\scriptstyle{4.18.5}\\ H^*R\Psi(V)_0&\longleftarrow&&\mathop{\longleftarrow}\limits^\sim&H^*(\overline U^*,V) \end{array} \]
from which 4.15 follows. One obtains 4.17 by replacing in it \(\Psi^{m}\) by \(\Psi^{mqu}\), the summation over \(\beta\) by a summation over the roots of unity and 4.18.5 by 4.18.6.

4.19. Let us deduce 4.15 and 4.17 from the particular case 4.18. Let \(u:Y\to X\) be a resolution of \((X,X_0)\), that is, a proper morphism, inducing an isomorphism \(Y^*\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim X^*\), such that \(Y\) is smooth and \((Y_0)_{\mathrm{red}}\) is a divisor with normal crossings of \(Y\). If \(V\) is quasi-unipotent along \(X_0\), its inverse image \(u^*V\) is quasi-unipotent along \(Y_0\).

For every coherent sheaf \(F\) on \(X\), let us denote by \(F^m\) or \(j_*^m j^*F\) the subsheaf of \(j_*j^*F\) formed of the meromorphic sections ; if \(f\) is the equation of \(X_0\), \(j_*^m j^*F\) is the inductive limit of the inductive system
\[ \tag{4.19.1} F\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^f F\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^f F\longrightarrow\cdots \]
The following lemma is proved in [2].

Lemma 4.19.2.

For every coherent sheaf \(F\) on \(X\), we have

\[ F^m\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim u_*(u^*F)^m \]

and \(R^iu_*(u^*F)^m=0\) for \(i>0\).

By Grauert's theorem, the \(R^iu_*u^*F\) are coherent sheaves. The kernels and cokernels of \(F\longrightarrow u_*u^*F\), and the \(R^iu_*F\) \((i>0)\), are zero outside \(X_0\), hence locally annihilated by a power of \(f\). Since \(u\) is proper, \(R^iu_*\) commutes with filtered inductive limits, in particular with the inductive limit (4.19.1), and (4.19.2) follows.
From 4.19.2 and 4.12 one deduces that, under the hypotheses of loc. cit. and again denoting by \(u_0\) the map from \(Y_0\) into \(X_0\) induced by \(u\), we have :

Lemma 4.19.3.

The morphisms

\[ \Psi_\eta^m(F)\longrightarrow u_{0*}\Psi_\eta^m(u^*F)\longrightarrow Ru_{0*}\Psi_\eta^m(u^*F) \]

are isomorphisms.

That the second arrow is an isomorphism means that \(R^iu_{0*}\Psi_\eta^m(u^*F)=0\) for \(i>0\). This follows from 4.12 and from \(R^iu_*(i'u^*F)^m=0\) for \(i>0\), which follows from \(R^iu_*(u^*F^m)=0\) for \(i>0\) and the base change theorem for the proper morphism \(u\).
To prove 4.15, it remains to contemplate the commutative diagram

\[ \begin{array}{ccc} \Psi_\eta^m(\Omega_{X^*}^*(V))&\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits_{(4.19.3)}&u_{0*}\Psi_\eta^m(\Omega_{Y^*}^*(u^*V))\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^\sim_{(4.19.3)}Ru_{0*}\Psi_\eta^m\Omega_{Y^*}^*(u^*V)\\ \downarrow&&\downarrow\scriptstyle{(4.18)}\\ R\Psi(V)&\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits_{(I.3.1;\,XIII\ 2.1.7.1)}&&Ru_{0*}R\Psi(u^*V) \end{array} \]
One proceeds in the same way for 4.17, replacing \(\Psi^m\) everywhere by \(\Psi^{mqu}\).
Remark 4.20. Theorem 4.13 provides an incarnation of the monodromy theorem at the level of complexes.
